The financial linear block is the part of Appendix C where small notation
choices become large model choices. It is therefore useful to write the row
ladder in source order. C.69 and C.70 linearize the two asset FOCs. C.71
linearizes the binding incentive constraint or, equivalently, the
capital-demand equation. C.72--C.74 linearize the shadow values of capital,
bonds, and net worth. C.75 linearizes the intermediary stochastic discount
factor. C.76 and C.77 then move from values and spreads to the law of motion
for aggregate intermediary net worth.

source_spans.text:
\paragraph{How financial-block choices travel through bank valuation.}
The financial block can now be read as a directed sequence:
\[
\begin{array}{c}
\lambda_{k,t},\ \mu_t
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
\nu_{k,t},\nu_{b,t}
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
\hbox{capital demand and incentive tightness}\\
\Downarrow\\
\Omega_t,\ r^k_t,\ r^b_t,\ r^d_t
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
\nu_{k,t},\nu_{b,t},\nu_{n,t}
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
\hbox{future bank demand}\\
\Downarrow\\
\hbox{surviving net worth}+\hbox{new banker entry}
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
N_t .
\end{array}
\]
This sequence explains why the live ambiguities are not isolated typos. The
deposit-return term in the bank discount factor changes how the bank values
future net worth, and that valuation enters the capital, bond, and net-worth
shadow values. The asset base used to capitalize entering bankers changes new
banker net worth and therefore aggregate bank net worth. The liquidity unit
choice changes both the bank capacity constraint and retained-earnings law.
The capital-return timing choice changes the payoff on bank-held capital
claims. The long-bond payoff convention changes the return on bank-held
government bonds. A defensible BGS reconstruction must therefore state these
choices as a connected financial-transition system, using Appendix C row
numbers as source addresses rather than as the explanation itself.

claims. The long-bond payoff convention changes the return on bank-held
government bonds. A defensible BGS reconstruction must therefore state these
choices as a connected financial-transition system, using Appendix C row
numbers as source addresses rather than as the explanation itself.

\paragraph{How financial-block choices travel through bank valuation.}
The financial block can now be read as a directed sequence:
\[
\begin{array}{c}
\lambda_{k,t},\ \mu_t
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
\nu_{k,t},\nu_{b,t}
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
\hbox{capital demand and incentive tightness}\\
\Downarrow\\
\Omega_t,\ r^k_t,\ r^b_t,\ r^d_t
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
\nu_{k,t},\nu_{b,t},\nu_{n,t}
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
\hbox{future bank demand}\\
\Downarrow\\
\hbox{surviving net worth}+\hbox{new banker entry}
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
N_t .
\end{array}
\]
This sequence explains why the live ambiguities are not isolated typos. The
deposit-return term in the bank discount factor changes how the bank values
future net worth, and that valuation enters the capital, bond, and net-worth
shadow values. The asset base used to capitalize entering bankers changes new
banker net worth and therefore aggregate bank net worth. The liquidity unit
choice changes both the bank capacity constraint and retained-earnings law.
The capital-return timing choice changes the payoff on bank-held capital
claims. The long-bond payoff convention changes the return on bank-held
government bonds. A defensible BGS reconstruction must therefore state these
choices as a connected financial-transition system, using Appendix C row
numbers as source addresses rather than as the explanation itself.

paragraphs.text: \subsection{A Row-By-Row Ladder For C.78--C.85}

Rows C.78--C.85 are short in the printed appendix, but they perform three
different tasks. C.78 is fiscal accounting. C.79 prices the long government
bond. C.80 is the lower-bound identity for the actual policy rate. C.81 is the
liquidity process in measured units. C.82--C.85 convert asset-market clearing
residuals into observed QE quantities. Mixing those roles is a common source
of false BGS reconstructions.

\paragraph{C.79: long-bond return.}
The level long-bond return row is
\begin{equation}
R^b_t=\frac{\xi+\kappa_bQ^b_t}{Q^b_{t-1}}.
\label{eq:bgs-c79-level-ladder}
\end{equation}
This is the same price-payoff logic as a consol or geometrically decaying
coupon bond: last period's price is the purchase price, and today's coupon plus
continuation price is the payoff. The first-order expansion is
\begin{equation}
R^b r^b_t
=\kappa_bQ^bq^b_t-R^bq^b_{t-1},
\qquad\hbox{so}\qquad
R^b(r^b_t+q^b_{t-1})=\kappa_bQ^bq^b_t.
\label{eq:bgs-c79-positive-derived-ladder}
\end{equation}
The printed C.79 extraction displays a negative left-hand side and uses a
coefficient symbol that collides with the fiscal-feedback coefficient in C.78.
The level row above shows what has to be true economically: \(q^b_{t-1}\) is
the inherited price in the denominator, \(q^b_t\) is the continuation-price
movement in the numerator, and the current-price coefficient is a bond-pricing
object. If a candidate uses the printed negative form, it must also explain how
that sign is reconciled with the level return definition and with the bond
shadow-value row C.73.

paragraphs.text:
\paragraph{What this ladder proves.}
C.78--C.85 form a measurement and policy bridge, not a new private-sector
optimization block. C.78 keeps government debt coherent. C.79 prices the long
bond that enters bank and household portfolios. C.80 separates the notional
policy rule from the actual constrained rate. C.81 distinguishes measured
liquidity from level liquidity in the bank equations. C.82--C.85 distinguish
central-bank purchase residuals from bank-held and household-held assets. A
candidate that conflates any of these roles can match some symbols in Appendix
C while still solving a different empirical model.
\section{BGS Shock Processes And Policy-Tool States}

BGS uses a larger shock system than the SW benchmark because it has both
standard macro disturbances and unconventional policy tools. The source
processes can be grouped as
\begin{align}
v_{u,t}&=\rho_uv_{u,t-1}+\epsilon^u_t,
&
z_t&=\rho_zz_{t-1}+\epsilon^z_t,\\
g_t&=\rho_gg_{t-1}+\epsilon^g_t+\rho_{gz}z_t,
&
v_{r,t}&=\rho_rv_{r,t-1}+\epsilon^r_t,\\
v_{i,t}&=\rho_iv_{i,t-1}+\epsilon^i_t,
&
v_{p,t}&=\rho_pv_{p,t-1}+\epsilon^p_t-\mu_p\epsilon^p_{t-1},\\
v_{w,t}&=\rho_wv_{w,t-1}+\epsilon^w_t-\mu_w\epsilon^w_{t-1},
&
v_{\lambda k,t}&=\rho_{\lambda k}v_{\lambda k,t-1}
+\epsilon^{\lambda k}_t,\\
\widetilde K_{cb,t}
&=\rho_{k,1}\widetilde K_{cb,t-1}
+\rho_{k,2}\widetilde K_{cb,t-2}
+\epsilon^{QEK}_t,\\
\widetilde B_{cb,t}
&=\rho_{b,1}\widetilde B_{cb,t-1}
+\rho_{b,2}\widetilde B_{cb,t-2}
+\epsilon^{QEB}_t,\\
\widetilde L_t
&=\rho_{cbl}\widetilde L_{t-1}+\epsilon^{CBL}_t.
\label{eq:bgs-shock-processes}
\end{align}
The first seven processes are familiar from the SW macro environment:
risk-premium, productivity, spending, monetary policy, investment, price
markup, and wage markup. The last four are BGS-specific or financial-policy
objects: capital divertibility, capital-claim purchases, government-bond
purchases, and central-bank liquidity. This grouping is useful because it
separates macro propagation shocks from balance-sheet and policy-tool states.
